does: This paper applies sparse autoencoders to decompose and interpret the internal representations learned by the language model backbone of CosyVoice3, a text-to-speech system that processes both text and speech tokens in a shared residual stream. The authors train BatchTopK sparse autoencoders and develop a modality-aware auto-interpretation pipeline that classifies recovered features based on whether they respond to text context, speech clips, or both modalities. They discover interpretable features corresponding to linguistic units (phonemes), paralinguistic phenomena (laughter), speaker characteristics (gender, accent), and speech properties (rate). Critically, they demonstrate causality through steering interventions in the SAE latent space, showing that manipulating individual features can dramatically alter output properties—increasing laughter probability from 0.02 to 0.79, flipping perceived speaker gender, and controlling speech rate while preserving semantic content.
connects: This work builds on the emerging line of research applying sparse autoencoders to interpret language model internals, extending recent successes in mechanistic interpretability from text-only models to the multimodal setting of speech synthesis. It responds to a key gap in understanding how LM backbones handle mixed modality inputs when text and speech representations share computational space. The paper combines techniques from mechanistic interpretability (SAE probing and feature discovery) with causal intervention methods, building on prior work demonstrating that SAE-recovered features are causally relevant for model behavior. The modality-aware auto-interpretation pipeline adapts existing feature labeling approaches to the distinct challenge of distinguishing text-conditioned versus speech-conditioned activations, addressing the specific architectural properties of modern TTS systems.
enables: This interpretability and steering capability opens multiple research directions in controllable speech synthesis and alignment of TTS systems. The discovery that individual SAE features causally control specific speech attributes suggests a new paradigm for fine-grained TTS control that bypasses traditional conditioning mechanisms, potentially enabling zero-shot style transfer and more nuanced voice control. The framework could extend to other multimodal language models processing mixed input types, providing a general methodology for understanding interleaved representations. Understanding how LMs organize speech and text jointly may inform efforts to make TTS systems more interpretable for auditing, detecting biases in speaker modeling, and ensuring speaker identity is appropriately controlled. Finally, the causal steering approach could support safer generation by identifying and manipulating features corresponding to unwanted attributes or harmful outputs.
